entities.name: BACKCROSS BREEDING
entities.description: A breeding method used for improvement of a pure line by addition of favorable alleles from a donor line

entities.name: PURE LINE
entities.description: A homozygous line used as the recipient in backcross breeding

entities.name: FAVORABLE ALLELES
entities.description: Specific versions of genes from a donor line that are desired for improvement

entities.name: DONOR LINE
entities.description: The source of favorable alleles in a backcross breeding program

entities.name: COMPUTER SIMULATION
entities.description: A technique used to investigate the potential of backcross breeding coupled with quantitative genetics theory

entities.name: QUANTITATIVE GENETICS THEORY
entities.description: The theoretical framework used in conjunction with computer simulation to study breeding

entities.name: HERITABILITY
entities.description: The proportion of phenotypic variation due to genetic factors, specifically among individual F2 plants

entities.name: NUMBER OF GENES
entities.description: The count of genes for which the donor line has a more favorable allele than the recipient

entities.name: RECIPIENT
entities.description: The line into which favorable alleles from a donor line are introduced

entities.name: POLLINATIONS
entities.description: The process of transferring pollen, with a minimum of 1,000 per backcross generation in the studied programs

entities.name: SELECTION AMONG FAMILIES
entities.description: The process of choosing specific families over three or more generations

entities.name: PROBABILITY OF FIXATION
entities.description: The criterion used to measure the effectiveness of the breeding program

entities.name: SELECTED TRAIT
entities.description: The specific characteristic being improved through the breeding program

entities.name: ADDITIVE EFFECTS
entities.description: The assumption that the effects of alleles add up to produce the phenotype

entities.name: INDEPENDENT ASSORTMENT
entities.description: The assumption that genes segregate independently during gamete formation
